_README: MOCK DATA for the /training course player. Structure: company/sessions/tracks/roster + modules + lessons. Each lesson has a `blocks` array (heading | text | list | callout | prompt | video). trackId 'all' = shown to everyone; otherwise the lesson only shows for that track (used for the hands-on 'apply it' lessons). To go live on a Google Sheet: store sessions/tracks/roster/modules as tabs, and lesson blocks as a markdown column converted on read. See lib/training.ts.

company.name: Rafanelli & Nahas
company.tagline: AI Fluency Program · built with Fieldwork AI

sessions.title: Session 1 — Foundations & Fluency
sessions.subtitle: Get fluent, set the ground rules, and get your first real win.

sessions.title: Session 2 — Applied Workflows & Governance
sessions.subtitle: Turn fluency into real R&N workflows you can trust and reuse.

blocks.text: Welcome. Over two half-days you'll go from "I've heard of AI" to confidently using it on real Rafanelli & Nahas work — leases, briefs, tenant notices, work orders, the things actually on your desk.

The goal isn't to turn you into a programmer. It's leverage: each person here doing more of their best work, with AI handling the repetitive parts. You stay in charge. AI does the first draft, the grunt work, the second pair of eyes.
Two simple rules for the whole program

items: Bring real work. Every exercise uses an actual R&N task, not a made-up one. You'll leave each session with something done.
You own the result. AI drafts; you decide. Nothing goes to a tenant, partner, or vendor without a human signing off.

blocks.title: Move at your own pace
blocks.text: Use the sidebar to jump between lessons. Your progress is saved on this device, so you can stop and pick up where you left off.

Before AI can help you, it needs context about R&N. The single highest-leverage thing you can do is build a short "company cheat sheet" — a document you paste in (or keep handy) so every AI conversation starts already knowing who you are.
Think of it like onboarding a sharp new hire who's never heard of R&N. What would they need to know to be useful on day one?
What goes in it

items: Who R&N is: privately held Boise CRE developer and long-hold owner/operator, ~15 people, 1M+ sq ft in the Treasure Valley.

Your buildings and entities: e.g. Capitol & Idaho (702 W. Idaho St) under 702 W. Idaho, LLC — Scott Schoenherr signs as Managing Member; River Quarry at Parkcenter (412 E. Parkcenter Blvd) under River Quarry at Parkcenter, LLC — Ron Nahas signs as Manager.
How R&N writes: clear, firm, never adversarial; documents follow a consistent structure and voice.
Your role and what you do day to day.
What's off-limits: tenant financials marked confidential, anything under an NDA. (More on this in the diligence lessons.)

blocks.title: Exercise — draft your cheat sheet (15 min)
items: Open a chat with Claude.
Ask: "Help me build a one-page company context document. Interview me with 5–7 questions about my business, then write it up." Answer its questions.
Read what it produces, fix anything wrong, and save it. This is your context for everything else in the program.

blocks.title: Starter prompt
blocks.text: I want to build a one-page company context document I can reuse with AI. Interview me with 5–7 short questions about my company, my role, our voice, our customers, and what's sensitive. Then write the document in plain, organized sections.
You don't need the math, but a working mental model helps you trust it in the right places and double-check it in the others.

Generative AI like Claude was trained on an enormous amount of text. From all that reading, it learned the patterns of language well enough to predict what should come next — which lets it write, summarize, explain, and reason through problems. It's not looking anything up in a database; it's generating a response from patterns.

blocks.label: Watch: How generative AI works (~12 min)

blocks.text: What that means in practice

items: It's genuinely strong at: drafting, rewriting, summarizing long documents, explaining things at different levels, organizing messy information, and being a thinking partner.
It's weaker at: knowing recent or local facts, exact figures, and anything it wasn't clearly told — where it may fill gaps with confident-sounding guesses.
It has no memory of your business unless you give it context (that's why the cheat sheet matters).

blocks.text: Rule of thumb: AI is brilliant at handling language and structure, and unreliable at facts you haven't given it. Lean on the first; verify the second.

lessons.title: The trust check: where AI gets things wrong

blocks.text: The fastest way to learn where to trust AI is to test it on something you already know cold — so you'll instantly spot a mistake.
The three failure modes to watch for

items: Hallucination — it invents a plausible-sounding fact, source, or citation that isn't real. Common with specific names, numbers, and references.
Knowledge cutoff — its training has a cutoff date, so recent rule changes, current pricing, or local specifics may be stale. Good models will caveat this; press them to.
Confident vagueness — it restates the obvious instead of addressing the actual hard part of your question.

blocks.title: Exercise — test it on your turf (15 min)
items: Pick a topic you know deeply (a lease clause, a building system, a local regulation, your own numbers).
Versatility test: ask AI to explain it three ways in one response — for a tenant, for a partner, for a new hire. Did the audience shifts actually land?
Hallucination test: ask it to name 2–3 real resources (a trade body, a publication, a supplier). Spot-check one. Does it actually exist?
Cutoff test: ask something time-sensitive or local. Does it caveat that its info may be outdated, or does it present a stale guess as fact?

blocks.text: When you want AI to avoid guessing, say so: "If you're not certain, say so and flag it — never invent a fact, name, or number." You'll use this discipline (we call it NEEDS REVIEW) all through the program.
Everything in this program runs on one simple framework: the 4D Framework. Four competencies — Delegation, Description, Discernment, Diligence — organized into two loops.
The inner loop — your day-to-day

Description and Discernment are the back-and-forth of a single AI conversation: you describe what you need, you evaluate what comes back, you refine. This is the loop you'll run dozens of times a day.
The outer loop — your bigger decisions
Delegation and Diligence are the wider judgment calls: deciding what AI should handle in the first place, and taking responsibility for how it's used. This loop runs less often but matters more.

blocks.title: The four moves
items: Delegation — decide what AI handles and what stays with you.
Description — communicate clearly what you need and how you want it approached.
Discernment — evaluate what AI gives back before acting on it.
Diligence — take full responsibility for how you use AI and own the final result.

blocks.text: It's a loop, not a checklist — you'll flow back and forth between these. That fluid movement is what AI fluency actually feels like.
Delegation is deciding, up front, what's a good fit for AI and what isn't. Get this right and everything downstream is easier.
Three buckets

items: AI can handle: standardized, documented, repeatable work — pulling terms out of a lease, formatting a notice, summarizing a report.
AI assists, human decides: AI drafts or organizes, a person reviews before anything leaves the building — a tenant email, an amendment draft, a vendor comparison.
Human should handle: high-stakes, relationship-sensitive, or complex judgment — negotiating a deal, resolving a real complaint, a hard conversation.

blocks.text: A good test: "If this were done imperfectly, what's the cost?" Low cost + repetitive = great for AI. High cost or high-trust = keep it close, or keep AI strictly in an assist role.
Most disappointing AI output is really a description problem. The fix is to be specific, like you're instructing a very capable but very literal new employee. Three things to describe:

items: Product — what you want back. The end result, the inputs you're giving, and the format (a table, a one-page brief, a draft email).
Process — how to approach it. The steps, the decision points ("if it's about pricing, use the price list; if it's custom, flag it"), and when to stop and ask you.
Performance — how it should behave. The tone, how to handle uncertainty ("if unsure, say so — never guess"), and the hard "never"s ("never promise a date," "never share another tenant's info").

blocks.title: Give it your context
blocks.text: Paste your company cheat sheet at the start. A described task plus real context is the difference between generic output and something that sounds like R&N.

blocks.title: Shape of a good description
blocks.text:
Context: [paste your cheat sheet]
Product: I need [end result] from [these inputs], formatted as [format].
Process: First do [X]. If [condition], then [Y]; otherwise flag it for me. Stop and ask if [Z].
Performance: Tone should be [tone]. If you're unsure, say so and flag it — never guess. Never [the hard nevers].
Discernment is evaluating what comes back before you act on it. This is where your expertise is irreplaceable — you can spot a wrong number or an off tone instantly.
Three things to check, every time

items: Accuracy — are the facts, figures, names, and dates right? Check them against your own records, not the AI's confidence.
Fit — does it sound like R&N? Right tone, right structure, nothing that contradicts how you do things?
Gaps & fabrications — did it quietly invent anything, or skip something it didn't actually have? Anything it guessed should be flagged, not buried.

blocks.text: Discernment turns AI from "impressive demo" into "reliable tool." If something's off, you don't start over — you describe the fix and loop again. Two or three rounds of refinement is normal and expected.
Diligence is taking full responsibility for how AI is used and what goes out the door under R&N's name. AI is a tool; accountability is always yours.
Three kinds of diligence

items: Creation — is this task appropriate for AI, and what could go wrong? Name the failure modes and a safeguard for each.
Deployment — how will you review outputs, when can you reduce oversight, and what would make you pause the whole thing?
Transparency — who needs to know AI was involved (tenants, partners, team), and how will you say so?

blocks.title: R&N's data ground rules
items: R&N's data is not used to train the AI, and files stay in OneDrive where they live.
Safe-to-use check: would you email this to a trusted professional, does the tenant know, is it normal business? Yes to all three → fine to run through AI.
Worth a pause: tenant financials marked confidential, anything under an NDA, or data you've promised a third party you'd handle a certain way.

blocks.text: We'll turn this into a written team policy in Session 2. For now: when in doubt, slow down and ask.

lessons.title: Your first win — a leadership task

blocks.text: Let's run the inner loop on something real to you: a decision, an update, or a piece of analysis you're sitting on right now.

blocks.title: Exercise — apply the 4D loop (20 min)
items: Delegation: pick a task where AI assists and you decide — e.g. pressure-testing a decision, drafting a partner/IC update, or sizing the ROI of a workflow.
Description: paste your cheat sheet, then describe the product, process, and performance you want.
Discernment: check every figure and claim against what you actually know. Flag anything it guessed.
Diligence: decide what (if anything) you'd be comfortable acting on, and what still needs your own work.

blocks.title: Pressure-test a decision
blocks.text:
[paste cheat sheet]
I'm weighing this decision: [decision]. Act as a skeptical partner. Give me the strongest case for, the strongest case against, the 3 assumptions it depends on, and what would have to be true for this to be a mistake. Be direct; flag anything you're unsure about.

blocks.title: Size the ROI of a workflow
blocks.text: Help me estimate ROI for automating this task: [task]. It takes about [time], happens [frequency], done by [who]; outsourced it costs [$]. Show time and dollars saved per month and per year, and the risk reduced. Show the math — don't inflate it.

lessons.title: Your first win — a lease task

blocks.text: Let's run the inner loop on a real lease document — the kind of work that normally eats a half-day.

blocks.title: Exercise — abstract a real lease (20 min)
items: Delegation: this is a great AI-handles / you-verify task — extracting documented terms.
Description: paste your cheat sheet, then use the prompt below on a lease or amendment you know well.
Discernment: check every NEEDS REVIEW flag — did it correctly refuse to guess? Verify the figures.
Diligence: note one thing it caught that saves you time, and anything you'd never let it finalize without review.

blocks.title: Extract operative lease terms
blocks.text:
You are a CRE lease analyst for R&N. [paste cheat sheet]
From the document below, extract: parties (full legal names + entity types), building/premises/suite, rentable area, term, base year, rent schedule, parking, security deposit, key dates, and exhibits. Output as a clean labeled list. Mark anything not clearly stated as NEEDS REVIEW. Do not guess.

[paste lease text]

blocks.title: Pull critical dates
blocks.text:
From the lease below, list every date-driven obligation: commencement, expiration, renewal/option windows, notice deadlines (days required + to whom), and rent-step dates. Table: Obligation | Date/Window | Notice required | Owner. Flag anything ambiguous as NEEDS REVIEW.

[paste lease text]

lessons.title: Your first win — a field task

blocks.text: Let's run the inner loop on real field work — turning rough notes into clean records, fast.

blocks.title: Exercise — clean up a work order (20 min)
items: Delegation: turning a messy note into a structured record is ideal AI-assist work.
Description: paste your cheat sheet, then use the prompt below on a recent note or photo description.
Discernment: confirm the priority and that it kept your facts straight — no invented details.
Diligence: compare it to how long this normally takes, and note anything you'd always double-check.

blocks.title: Field note → work-order entry
blocks.text:
Turn the rough note below into a structured work-order entry: Building/Unit, Issue, Priority (Low/Med/High/Emergency), What I observed, Action taken/needed, Parts or vendor required, Follow-up. Keep my facts; don't invent details. If priority isn't clear, ask.

NOTE:
[paste or dictate your note]

blocks.title: First-pass equipment diagnostic
blocks.text: Act as a building-systems technician. A [equipment type, make/model if known] is showing [symptoms]. Give the 5 most likely causes ranked by probability, the safe checks I can do myself, and the point where I should stop and call a licensed pro. Safety first — flag anything I should not touch.

lessons.title: Recap & your cheat sheet in action

blocks.text: Welcome back. Quick recap of Session 1, then we get to work building real R&N workflows.

items: Your company cheat sheet gives AI context — keep it handy and paste it at the start of serious work.
AI is strong on language and structure, weaker on facts you haven't given it — verify those.
The 4D loop: Delegate the right task, Describe it clearly, Discern what comes back, take Diligence for the result.

blocks.title: Warm-up (5 min)
blocks.text: Pull up your cheat sheet from Session 1 and read it with fresh eyes. Update anything that's changed or that you'd now add. We'll use it throughout today.
Before building anything, find the right thing to build. The best first workflow is repetitive, frequent, fairly standardized, and low-stakes if it's slightly off.

blocks.title: Exercise 1 — audit, categorize, prioritize (20 min)
items: Audit: list 5–10 tasks from your past week that felt repetitive or time-consuming. For each, note how often it happens, how long it takes, how standardized it is, and the cost if done imperfectly.
Categorize: sort them into AI can handle / AI assists, human decides / Human should handle.
Prioritize: pick the one task from the first two buckets that would save you the most time, and write one sentence on why it's your top pick.

blocks.text: Examples by role — Ops: drafting tenant notices, abstracting leases, prepping critical-date lists. Leadership: recurring partner/IC updates, first-pass market scans. Maintenance: work-order write-ups, vendor request emails, bid comparisons.
Take the task you prioritized and describe it precisely using the three parts of Description — Product, Process, Performance. This is the blueprint AI executes against.

items: Product: the end result, the inputs it receives, and the outputs it should produce.
Process: the step-by-step logic, the decision points, when to escalate to a human, and what documents it needs (FAQ, price list, cheat sheet).
Performance: the tone, how to handle uncertainty ("if unsure, flag — never guess"), and the hard nevers.

blocks.title: Exercise 2 — write it, then test it (25 min)
items: Write your Product / Process / Performance descriptions (2–3 sentences each).
Share them with AI along with 3–5 real R&N examples from your work.
Evaluate: did it categorize right? Accurate and on-tone? Did it fabricate anything?
Refine. Plan on 2–3 rounds before it feels reliable — that's normal.

blocks.title: Test-run scaffold
blocks.text:
[paste cheat sheet]
Here is an automation I want to build.
PRODUCT: [what you want back, inputs, outputs]
PROCESS: [steps, decision points, when to escalate]
PERFORMANCE: [tone, how to handle uncertainty, the nevers]

Here are 3–5 real examples to run it on:
[paste examples]

Run each example, then tell me where my description needs to be sharper.
Before you rely on a workflow, think through what could go wrong and how you'll catch it. Diligence is what makes automation safe to trust.

blocks.title: Exercise 3 — three kinds of diligence (20 min)
items: Creation: why is this task a good fit? List 3 failure modes (e.g. wrong pricing, templated reply to a complaint, mixing up tenants) and a safeguard for each.
Deployment: will you review every output or sample? When would you reduce oversight, and what evidence would you need? What would make you pause it entirely?
Transparency: who needs to know AI is involved, and how will you tell them?

blocks.title: Automation Readiness Statement
blocks.text: Wrap it up in 3–5 sentences: what you're automating, why it's appropriate, the safeguards in place, and how you'll be transparent. Keep it as a reference for you and your team.
A short, honest AI policy scales your judgment — so the whole team works from the same understanding instead of making it up case by case.

blocks.title: Exercise — write a one-page policy (20 min)
items: Reflect: What tasks are you now confident handing to AI? What will you always keep human? What do tenants and your team need to know?
Build it with AI: share your cheat sheet and those answers, and ask for a one-page policy with three sections — What we use AI for / What stays human / How we stay accountable.
Stress-test: if a tenant asked "do you use AI in your business?", does this give you a clear, honest answer? Fix anything you softened.

blocks.title: Draft the policy
blocks.text:
[paste cheat sheet]
Here's how I think about AI in our work:
- Confident to hand off: [list]
- Always human: [list]
- What people should know: [list]

Draft a one-page AI use policy for R&N with three sections: "What we use AI for" (approved tasks/tools), "What stays human" (and why), and "How we stay accountable" (oversight, transparency, what happens when something goes wrong). Plain language that sounds like us.

lessons.title: Staying the human in the loop

blocks.text: The point of all this isn't to remove people — it's to free you for the work only people can do.

blocks.title: Key takeaways
items: Being the human in the loop is more than oversight — you decide what problems AI should even solve.
Avoid dependency through understanding, not avoidance: if you can explain what the AI is doing, that's a good sign.
AI should free you for more human work, not less — the personal outreach, the relationship, the judgment call.
Decide as a team what happens with the time AI saves. Set that norm early.
Your AI policy is how this understanding scales as R&N grows.

lessons.title: Your task this week & what's next

blocks.text: Fluency comes from reps. Before you leave, commit to one real task you'll tackle with AI this week — your practice ground.

blocks.title: Final assignment
items: Pick one concrete task waiting for you (a draft, a market scan, a data summary, an onboarding doc, a plan).
Run the full 4D loop on it: Delegation → Description → Discernment → Diligence.
Afterward, jot down what worked, what needed correction, and which competency needs more practice.

blocks.text: Keep going

items: Keep a running doc of the prompts and workflows that work for you — so you're not starting from scratch, and teammates can pick them up.
Blueprints: step-by-step guides for common tasks at claude.com/resources/use-cases.
Stay current: rules around AI in business are evolving (FTC guidance, state privacy laws). Revisit these principles as things change.
Questions between sessions? gabe@getfieldworkai.com — that's what office hours are for.

blocks.title: You're done.
blocks.text: You've built a cheat sheet, learned the 4D Framework, mapped your opportunities, built and stress-tested a workflow, and written your AI policy. That's AI fluency. Now go get the reps.
